intitule: Listening Comprehension: Gender Discrimination in the Workplace

enonce.texte:
You will listen to a short testimony from a young Cameroonian woman named Amira. She is talking about her experience looking for a job after university. Listen carefully and answer the questions that follow.

Script for the teacher to read aloud:
"My name is Amira. I graduated from the University of Yaoundé with a degree in Civil Engineering. I was the top of my class. After six months of searching, I finally got an interview with a big construction company. The interview went perfectly. I answered all their technical questions. At the end, the manager, Mr. Atangana, said, 'You are brilliant, Amira. But this is a construction site. It's a man's world. It's very physical. Are you sure you can handle the long hours and the pressure? We are worried about investing in you if you plan to get married and have children soon.' I felt humiliated. They gave the job to a male colleague who had lower grades than me. He is now my boss, and I am still looking for work. This is the reality for many educated young women in our country. Our rights are on paper, but in people's minds, the old ideas remain."
images: A professional illustration of a young African woman in a business suit, holding a degree certificate, looking at a construction site in the background with a disappointed expression. The scene conveys a feeling of injustice and blocked opportunity.

questions.texte: Identify the main problem Amira is facing.
questions.reponse: Amira is facing gender discrimination in the workplace/job market.

questions.texte: List the two specific arguments Mr. Atangana used to justify not hiring Amira.
questions.reponse: 1. That a construction site is 'a man's world' and too physical. 2. That they were worried about investing in her if she plans to get married and have children soon.

questions.texte: Explain why Amira felt humiliated by the manager's questions.
questions.reponse: She felt humiliated because her qualifications (being top of her class and answering all technical questions) were ignored. She was judged not on her skills but on her gender and potential future family plans, which are irrelevant to her ability to do the job.

questions.texte: Analyze the contradiction Amira points out in her final sentence ('Our rights are on paper, but in people's minds, the old ideas remain.').
questions.reponse: Amira identifies a contradiction between the law and reality. 'On paper' refers to the Cameroonian constitution and laws that guarantee gender equality and prohibit discrimination. However, 'in people's minds' refers to persistent social and cultural prejudices. The law exists, but it is not effectively enforced or internalized by people like Mr. Atangana, making the legal rights meaningless in practice.

questions.texte: Propose two concrete actions that the Cameroonian government could take to solve the problem Amira describes.
questions.reponse: 1. Implement and enforce strict penalties for companies that practice gender discrimination, such as heavy fines or suspension of their operating licenses. 2. Launch a mandatory national awareness and training campaign for all HR managers and company directors on unconscious bias and the legal consequences of discrimination.

intitule: Community Action: Organizing a School Awareness Campaign

enonce.texte:
After listening to Amira's testimony, your English class has decided to take action. Your principal has asked your class to create a 10-minute presentation for the school assembly next month on the theme: 'Equality of Opportunity: For a Meritocratic Cameroon.'

You must use the information from the listening exercise to convince your fellow students that discrimination is a real problem and to propose solutions. Your goal is to write the script for a short, impactful scene or monologue to be performed by your classmates.
images: A group of diverse high school students (boys and girls) in a classroom in Cameroon, brainstorming ideas on a large piece of paper. One student is writing 'EQUALITY' at the top. The atmosphere is engaged and collaborative.

questions.texte: Draft a 150-word monologue for a character (like Amira) who will speak directly to the students. In this monologue, you must: (a) summarize the problem using reported speech, (b) use at least two rhetorical questions, and (c) end with a clear call to action using an imperative.
questions.reponse: (Example of a good monologue) 'Hello everyone. My name is... Amira. I want to tell you about a problem we all know exists. The manager at my job interview said that a construction site is a man's world. He asked if I was sure I could handle the pressure. He told me they were worried I would leave to have children. What does that have to do with my grades? What does that have to do with my skills? I passed my exam. I was the best. But he gave the job to a man. So, I ask you: Is this the Cameroon we want? Is this your definition of merit? No. So, I say this: Stand up. Speak out. Refuse to accept 'that's just how it is.' Together, let's build a country where your diploma, not your gender, decides your future.'

questions.texte: Write a short dialogue (8-10 exchanges) between two students, Paul and Christelle. Paul argues that discrimination is not a big issue and that 'maybe the man was just better for the job.' Christelle must counter each of Paul's arguments using facts and logical reasoning derived from Amira's testimony.
questions.reponse: (Example of a good dialogue) Paul: 'Christelle, I don't get the big deal. Maybe the man was just stronger.' Christelle: 'Paul, Amira was top of her class. He had lower grades. Strength isn't the main skill for an engineer; it's intelligence.' Paul: 'But the manager said it's a physical job.' Christelle: 'Then they should have a physical test for everyone, not assume a woman can't do it. That's a stereotype.' Paul: 'And the thing about marriage? That's just a real worry for a company.' Christelle: 'It's illegal discrimination. They don't ask men that question. It assumes a woman's career is less important. Her qualifications prove she's serious.' Paul: 'Okay, okay. So what's the solution?' Christelle: 'The solution is to judge the person, not the gender. Report companies who do this. That's how we create real meritocracy.'
